text: The reverse smile trick. How to transform a double stapled into a single stapled colorectal anastomosis. The double stapled Knight-Griffin technique is the most commonly adopted for left-sided colorectal anastomosis. Two major drawbacks of this anastomosis are identified. The multiple stapler firings that may be needed for rectal transection and the formation of intersecting staple lines leading to the so-called dog ears or tennis racket. These are regarded as two risk factors for anastomotic leak.

There are several ways to perform a double stapled anastomosis. The first one is to position the spike of the transgenal circular stapler in the middle of the linear staple line, letting the spike perforate the closed rectum right in the middle third of the staple line. At the end, the anastomosis would have two dog ears and two intersecting staple lines. Another option is to orientate the profile of the circular stapler towards an extremity of the linear staple line. In this case, a so-called tennis racket would result with only one point of intersection of the staple lines. In the here presented reverse smile trick, the spike perforates the bowel just below the middle point of the linear staple line. The circular stapler is shifted upwards, forming a reverse smile with the linear staple line. The anastomosis that results from this will be formed by only one circular staple line with no intersecting points.

The video demonstrates the insertion of a circular stapler through the anus, advancing it through the rectum with assistance. The assistant stretches the rectum to facilitate the insertion, ensuring the stapler is positioned correctly for a single stapled anastomosis.

Under visual control, the surgeon perforates the bowel with the spike of the circular stapler in its central third, just below the staple line. The anvil is approximated and connected to the stapler. Tension is released, allowing the lateral thirds of the linear staple line to approximate below the anvil's axis, forming a reverse smile. The circular stapler is then closed completely, incorporating the linear staple line.

This drawing clarifies once again the trick. Both donuts are complete as demonstrated by these pictures. We can appreciate the completeness of the stapler line in the following pictures. In conclusion, this easy trick allows to transform a double stapled into a single stapled anastomosis, avoiding intersecting staple lines and possibly reducing the risk of a leak.
